hypothyroidism.epidemiology: Women > men (5-8:1). Peak age 30-50. Risk factors: family history of autoimmune disease, prior thyroid surgery/radiation, lithium or amiodarone use, iodine deficiency.
hypothyroidism.pathophysiology: Most commonly autoimmune destruction of thyroid (Hashimoto thyroiditis) with lymphocytic infiltration and anti-TPO antibodies. Decreased T4/T3 production leads to increased TSH via loss of negative feedback. Results in decreased basal metabolic rate affecting all organ systems.
classic_presentation: delayed relaxation of deep tendon reflexes
hypothyroidism.diagnostic_criteria: Elevated TSH with low free T4 confirms overt primary hypothyroidism. Subclinical: elevated TSH with normal free T4. Anti-TPO antibodies confirm autoimmune etiology.
cant_miss_features: myxedema coma (altered mental status, hypothermia, bradycardia)
hypothyroidism.typical_course: Gradual onset over months to years. Without treatment, progressive metabolic slowing. Rarely progresses to myxedema coma (mortality 30-60%). With levothyroxine replacement, symptoms resolve over weeks to months.

hyperthyroidism.epidemiology: Women > men (5-10:1). Graves disease peaks age 20-40. Toxic multinodular goiter more common in elderly. Prevalence ~1%.
hyperthyroidism.pathophysiology: Graves disease: TSH receptor-stimulating antibodies cause unregulated thyroid hormone production. Toxic nodular goiter: autonomous thyroid hormone secretion from hyperplastic nodules. Excess T3/T4 increases basal metabolic rate, beta-adrenergic sensitivity, and bone turnover.
hyperthyroidism.diagnostic_criteria: Suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and/or free T3. Radioactive iodine uptake scan differentiates Graves (diffuse uptake) from thyroiditis (low uptake) and toxic nodule (focal uptake).
cant_miss_features: thyroid storm (fever, tachycardia >140, altered mental status, high mortality)
hyperthyroidism.typical_course: Graves disease may have relapsing-remitting course. Without treatment, risk of thyroid storm, cardiac arrhythmias, osteoporosis. Treatment: antithyroid drugs (methimazole), radioactive iodine ablation, or surgery.

cushing_syndrome.epidemiology: Most cases iatrogenic (exogenous glucocorticoids). Endogenous: 2-3 per million/year. Women > men (3:1) for pituitary-dependent. Peak age 25-40.
cushing_syndrome.pathophysiology: Chronic cortisol excess from exogenous steroids, pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma (Cushing disease, 70%), ectopic ACTH (15%), or adrenal tumor (15%). Cortisol excess causes protein catabolism, gluconeogenesis, lipogenesis, immunosuppression, and mineralocorticoid effects.
cushing_syndrome.diagnostic_criteria: At least two positive screening tests (24h urine cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test). Then determine ACTH-dependent vs independent. MRI pituitary or CT adrenals based on ACTH level.
cant_miss_features: adrenal crisis if tumor removed without glucocorticoid taper
cushing_syndrome.typical_course: Without treatment, progressive morbidity and 5-year mortality of 50%. Pituitary surgery curative in 65-90% of Cushing disease. Adrenal adenoma: surgery curative.

addison_disease.epidemiology: Prevalence ~1 in 10,000. Peak age 30-50. Women slightly more affected. Most common cause in developed world: autoimmune adrenalitis (80%). Developing world: tuberculosis.
addison_disease.pathophysiology: Destruction of >90% of adrenal cortex leading to deficiency of cortisol, aldosterone, and adrenal androgens. Autoimmune adrenalitis involves T-cell mediated destruction. Loss of cortisol removes negative feedback, causing ACTH elevation (and hyperpigmentation via MSH co-secretion). Aldosterone deficiency causes sodium wasting and potassium retention.
addison_disease.diagnostic_criteria: Morning cortisol <3 mcg/dL is diagnostic. Cortisol 3-18: perform cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — failure to reach cortisol >18 mcg/dL at 30-60 min confirms diagnosis. Elevated ACTH confirms primary. 21-hydroxylase antibodies confirm autoimmune.
cant_miss_features: adrenal crisis (hypotension, shock, altered consciousness — triggered by infection/stress/surgery)
addison_disease.typical_course: Insidious onset over months. Without treatment, eventually fatal from adrenal crisis. With physiologic glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement, normal life expectancy with stress-dose steroid education.

diabetic_ketoacidosis.epidemiology: Annual incidence 4-8 per 1000 persons with diabetes. More common in type 1 diabetes but occurs in type 2 (especially in African Americans, Hispanic). Common precipitants: infection, insulin omission, new-onset T1DM, MI, medications (SGLT2 inhibitors).
diabetic_ketoacidosis.pathophysiology: Absolute or relative insulin deficiency with counter-regulatory hormone excess leads to hyperglycemia (hepatic gluconeogenesis, decreased glucose uptake), lipolysis, and hepatic ketogenesis (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate). Ketoacids consume bicarbonate causing high anion gap metabolic acidosis. Osmotic diuresis causes volume depletion and electrolyte losses.
diabetic_ketoacidosis.diagnostic_criteria: Triad: hyperglycemia (>250 mg/dL), metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, bicarb <18), and ketonemia/ketonuria. Classify severity: mild (pH 7.25-7.30), moderate (pH 7.00-7.24), severe (pH <7.00).
cant_miss_features: cerebral edema (especially pediatric, from overly rapid correction)
diabetic_ketoacidosis.typical_course: Develops over hours to 1-2 days. Mortality 1-5% (higher in elderly, with comorbidities). Treatment: IV fluids, insulin drip, potassium replacement, bicarbonate if pH <6.9. Resolves in 12-36 hours with treatment.

hyperosmolar_hyperglycemic_state.epidemiology: Typically type 2 diabetes, elderly. Mortality 5-20% (higher than DKA). Precipitants: infection, stroke, MI, medications (steroids, thiazides).
hyperosmolar_hyperglycemic_state.pathophysiology: Relative insulin deficiency sufficient to prevent ketosis but insufficient to prevent hyperglycemia. Severe hyperglycemia causes profound osmotic diuresis, dehydration, and hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg). Average fluid deficit 8-10 liters.
hyperosmolar_hyperglycemic_state.diagnostic_criteria: Glucose >600 mg/dL, serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.30, bicarbonate >18, absent/minimal ketones, altered sensorium.
cant_miss_features: cerebral edema from overly rapid correction
hyperosmolar_hyperglycemic_state.typical_course: Develops over days to weeks. Higher mortality than DKA. Treatment: aggressive IV fluids (primary), insulin, electrolyte replacement. Recovery over 3-5 days.

pheochromocytoma.epidemiology: Rare, 2-8 per million/year. Peak age 40-50. Rule of 10s: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal (paraganglioma), 10% malignant, 10% familial. Associated with MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDH mutations.
pheochromocytoma.pathophysiology: Catecholamine-secreting tumor of chromaffin cells (adrenal medulla or extra-adrenal paraganglia). Episodic or sustained release of epinephrine/norepinephrine causes paroxysmal hypertension, tachycardia, and metabolic effects.
pheochromocytoma.diagnostic_criteria: Plasma free metanephrines (sensitivity 96-100%). If elevated >3x ULN, proceed to imaging (CT/MRI adrenals). 24h urine metanephrines for confirmation. MIBG scan for localization and metastatic disease.
cant_miss_features: hypertensive crisis during surgery/anesthesia if undiagnosed
must alpha-block before beta-block (phenoxybenzamine before propranolol)
pheochromocytoma.typical_course: Paroxysmal symptoms may occur for years before diagnosis. Surgical resection curative in >90% of benign tumors. Must be alpha-blocked preoperatively. 10% malignant with 5-year survival ~50%.

primary_hyperparathyroidism.epidemiology: Prevalence 1-2 per 1000. Women > men (3:1). Peak age 50-60 (postmenopausal women). 85% single adenoma, 15% hyperplasia, <1% carcinoma.
primary_hyperparathyroidism.pathophysiology: Autonomous PTH secretion from parathyroid adenoma (most common) causes increased bone resorption, increased renal calcium reabsorption, increased 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D synthesis (increasing intestinal calcium absorption), and renal phosphorus wasting.
primary_hyperparathyroidism.diagnostic_criteria: Elevated calcium with elevated or inappropriately normal PTH (should be suppressed if calcium is high). Exclude familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (24h urine calcium/creatinine clearance ratio >0.02). Sestamibi scan or ultrasound for localization pre-surgery.
cant_miss_features: hypercalcemic crisis (calcium >14, altered mental status, renal failure)
primary_hyperparathyroidism.typical_course: Usually indolent. Surgery (parathyroidectomy) curative in >95%. Observation with monitoring appropriate for mild asymptomatic disease in some patients.

hypoparathyroidism.epidemiology: Most common cause: surgical (post-thyroidectomy or parathyroidectomy). Autoimmune rare. Prevalence ~37 per 100,000.
hypoparathyroidism.pathophysiology: Insufficient PTH leads to decreased bone resorption, decreased renal calcium reabsorption, decreased 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D synthesis. Results in hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia.
hypoparathyroidism.diagnostic_criteria: Low calcium with low or inappropriately normal PTH. Exclude magnesium deficiency (can cause functional hypoparathyroidism). Must confirm on repeat testing.
cant_miss_features: cardiac arrhythmias (prolonged QT, torsades de pointes)
hypoparathyroidism.typical_course: Post-surgical: may be transient (days-weeks) or permanent. Treatment: calcium and calcitriol supplementation lifelong. PTH replacement (natpara) available for refractory cases.

siadh.epidemiology: Common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Causes: CNS disorders (stroke, trauma, infection), pulmonary disease (pneumonia, TB), malignancy (small cell lung cancer), medications (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide), postoperative state.
siadh.pathophysiology: Inappropriate continued secretion of ADH despite low serum osmolality, causing free water retention, dilutional hyponatremia, and concentrated urine. Euvolemic because excess water is partially excreted via pressure natriuresis.
siadh.diagnostic_criteria: Hyponatremia with low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg), urine Na >40, euvolemic, normal adrenal and thyroid function. Diagnosis of exclusion.
cant_miss_features: osmotic demyelination syndrome from overly rapid correction (>10-12 mEq/L in 24h)
siadh.typical_course: Depends on cause. Drug-induced: resolves with medication change. Malignancy-associated: treat underlying cancer. Treatment: fluid restriction, salt tablets, vasopressin receptor antagonists (tolvaptan), 3% saline for severe symptoms.

diabetes_insipidus.epidemiology: Central DI: pituitary surgery, head trauma, tumors, idiopathic (30%), autoimmune. Nephrogenic DI: lithium (most common drug cause), hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hereditary (X-linked).
diabetes_insipidus.pathophysiology: Central: insufficient ADH production/secretion. Nephrogenic: renal resistance to ADH. Both cause inability to concentrate urine, leading to polyuria (3-20 L/day) and compensatory polydipsia.
diabetes_insipidus.diagnostic_criteria: Water deprivation test: failure to concentrate urine (remains <300 mOsm/kg). Desmopressin challenge distinguishes central (urine concentrates >50%) from nephrogenic (no response). Copeptin level emerging as diagnostic.
cant_miss_features: severe hypernatremia with seizures/coma (especially in patients who cannot access water)
underlying pituitary tumor or infiltrative disease
diabetes_insipidus.typical_course: Central DI: post-surgical may be transient (triphasic response). Treatment: desmopressin. Nephrogenic DI: remove offending agent, thiazides paradoxically reduce urine volume, amiloride for lithium-induced.

acromegaly.epidemiology: Rare, prevalence ~60 per million. Equal sex distribution. Mean age at diagnosis 40-45 (delayed 5-10 years from symptom onset). >95% pituitary GH-secreting adenoma.
acromegaly.pathophysiology: Excess growth hormone stimulates hepatic IGF-1 production, causing soft tissue, bone, and organ growth. GH also has diabetogenic effects (insulin resistance) and causes sodium/water retention.
classic_presentation: enlarged hands and feet (ring/shoe size increase)
key_labs: failure of GH suppression on oral glucose tolerance test (GH >1 ng/mL)
acromegaly.diagnostic_criteria: Elevated IGF-1 for age/sex. Confirmed by failure to suppress GH to <1 ng/mL (or <0.4 with ultrasensitive assay) after 75g oral glucose load. MRI pituitary for adenoma.
acromegaly.typical_course: Insidious progression over years-decades. Untreated: 2-3x increased mortality from cardiovascular disease. Treatment: transsphenoidal surgery (cure 80-90% for microadenomas), somatostatin analogs (octreotide), GH receptor antagonist (pegvisomant).

iron_deficiency_anemia.epidemiology: Most common anemia worldwide. Premenopausal women (menstrual losses), children (rapid growth, dietary insufficiency), elderly (GI blood loss, poor absorption). Prevalence ~15% of world population.
iron_deficiency_anemia.pathophysiology: Iron depletion progresses through stages: depleted stores (low ferritin) → iron-deficient erythropoiesis (low transferrin saturation, rising TIBC) → iron deficiency anemia (microcytic hypochromic RBCs, low hemoglobin). Causes: blood loss (GI, menstrual), decreased absorption (celiac, gastric bypass), increased demand (pregnancy, growth).
key_labs: microcytic hypochromic anemia (low MCV, low MCH, low MCHC)
iron_deficiency_anemia.diagnostic_criteria: Ferritin <15 ng/mL is diagnostic (sensitivity 59%, specificity 99%). Ferritin <30 with clinical suspicion also diagnostic. In inflammation, ferritin <100 with transferrin saturation <20% suggests iron deficiency.
mimics: thalassemia trait (low MCV, normal/high ferritin, normal RDW)
cant_miss_features: underlying GI malignancy (must scope men and postmenopausal women)
iron_deficiency_anemia.typical_course: Responds to oral iron in 1-2 months (reticulocyte response in 7-10 days). Ferritin normalizes in 3-6 months. IV iron if oral-intolerant or malabsorptive. Must identify and treat underlying cause.

vitamin_b12_deficiency.epidemiology: Prevalence 3-5% in adults, up to 20% in elderly. Causes: pernicious anemia (autoimmune gastritis), malabsorption (celiac, Crohn's, gastric bypass), strict veganism, medications (metformin, PPIs).
vitamin_b12_deficiency.pathophysiology: B12 is required for methionine synthase (homocysteine→methionine) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (methylmalonyl-CoA→succinyl-CoA). Deficiency impairs DNA synthesis causing megaloblastic anemia and demyelination of dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts (subacute combined degeneration).
key_labs: elevated methylmalonic acid (specific for B12 vs folate)
vitamin_b12_deficiency.diagnostic_criteria: Serum B12 <200 pg/mL with clinical findings. If B12 200-400 (borderline), elevated methylmalonic acid and homocysteine confirm deficiency. Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies confirm pernicious anemia (specific but low sensitivity).
mimics: folate deficiency (also macrocytic, but no neurologic symptoms, normal MMA)
cant_miss_features: neurologic symptoms can occur without anemia
vitamin_b12_deficiency.typical_course: Neurologic symptoms may precede anemia. Hematologic response to B12 replacement within days (reticulocytosis). Neurologic improvement over months but may be incomplete if advanced. Lifelong B12 supplementation needed for pernicious anemia.

folate_deficiency.epidemiology: Causes: dietary insufficiency (alcoholism, poverty), malabsorption (celiac, tropical sprue), increased demand (pregnancy, hemolytic anemias), medications (methotrexate, trimethoprim, phenytoin).
folate_deficiency.pathophysiology: Folate is essential for one-carbon transfer reactions in DNA/RNA synthesis. Deficiency causes megaloblastic changes in rapidly dividing cells. Unlike B12 deficiency, does not cause neurologic damage.
folate_deficiency.diagnostic_criteria: Low serum folate with macrocytic anemia and elevated homocysteine. Normal methylmalonic acid distinguishes from B12 deficiency. Must always check B12 before treating folate (folate can mask B12 deficiency hematologically while neurologic damage progresses).
folate_deficiency.typical_course: Rapid hematologic response to folate supplementation (1-5 mg/day). Body stores depleted in 4 months (vs 3-5 years for B12).

hemolytic_anemia.epidemiology: Diverse causes: autoimmune (warm AIHA, cold agglutinin), hereditary (sickle cell, thalassemia, G6PD, spherocytosis), microangiopathic (TTP/HUS, DIC), mechanical (prosthetic valves), medications, infections (malaria).
hemolytic_anemia.pathophysiology: Accelerated RBC destruction (intravascular or extravascular) exceeding bone marrow compensatory capacity. Extravascular: RBC destruction in spleen/liver (spherocytes, positive DAT in AIHA). Intravascular: RBC lysis in circulation (hemoglobinuria, schistocytes in TMA).
hemolytic_anemia.diagnostic_criteria: Evidence of increased RBC destruction (low haptoglobin, elevated LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin) + evidence of compensatory erythropoiesis (reticulocytosis). Then classify by mechanism: DAT, peripheral smear, specific testing.
mimics: ineffective erythropoiesis (megaloblastic anemia, MDS — high LDH/bilirubin but low reticulocytes)
cant_miss_features: TTP (pentad: MAHA, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, neurologic symptoms, fever — do NOT give platelets)
hemolytic_anemia.typical_course: Depends on cause. AIHA: steroids first-line, rituximab/splenectomy for refractory. Hereditary: supportive, splenectomy for spherocytosis. Microangiopathic: treat underlying cause (plasma exchange for TTP).

disseminated_intravascular_coagulation.epidemiology: Occurs in setting of underlying disease: sepsis (most common), trauma, malignancy (acute promyelocytic leukemia, solid tumors), obstetric complications (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism), transfusion reactions.
disseminated_intravascular_coagulation.pathophysiology: Systemic activation of coagulation via tissue factor exposure, leading to widespread fibrin deposition in microvasculature (organ damage), consumption of platelets and clotting factors (bleeding), and secondary fibrinolysis (elevated D-dimer, FDPs).
classic_presentation: bleeding from multiple sites (venipuncture, surgical, mucosal)
disseminated_intravascular_coagulation.diagnostic_criteria: ISTH DIC scoring system: platelets (<100=1, <50=2), D-dimer (moderate=2, severe=3), prolonged PT (3-6sec=1, >6sec=2), fibrinogen (<100=1). Score ≥5 = overt DIC.
mimics: severe liver disease (similar labs but chronic)
cant_miss_features: acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) — treat with ATRA immediately
disseminated_intravascular_coagulation.typical_course: Mortality 40-80% depending on underlying cause. Treatment: treat underlying trigger, supportive with platelets/FFP/cryoprecipitate if actively bleeding or before procedures, heparin in chronic compensated DIC.

ttp_hus.epidemiology: TTP: rare (3-4 per million/year), women > men, peak age 30-50. Acquired (autoantibody to ADAMTS13, 95%) or hereditary. HUS: typical/STEC-HUS in children (E. coli O157:H7), atypical HUS from complement dysregulation.
ttp_hus.pathophysiology: TTP: ADAMTS13 deficiency causes inability to cleave ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers, leading to platelet-rich microthrombi. HUS: endothelial injury (Shiga toxin or complement-mediated) causes microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and renal injury.
ttp_hus.diagnostic_criteria: TTP: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + ADAMTS13 <10%. Do not wait for ADAMTS13 result to initiate plasma exchange if clinical suspicion high. HUS: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI, often preceded by diarrheal illness in children.
cant_miss_features: do NOT transfuse platelets in TTP (worsens thrombosis)
emergent plasma exchange for TTP (mortality >90% without treatment)
avoid antibiotics in STEC-HUS (may worsen toxin release)
ttp_hus.typical_course: TTP: medical emergency. Plasma exchange is mainstay (reduces mortality from >90% to 10-20%). Caplacizumab (anti-vWF) adjunctive. Relapse rate 30-50%. STEC-HUS: supportive care, most children recover. Atypical HUS: eculizumab (anti-C5).

multiple_myeloma.epidemiology: Median age at diagnosis 69. More common in males, African Americans (2:1). Prevalence ~30 per 100,000. Preceded by MGUS (1%/year progression to myeloma).
multiple_myeloma.pathophysiology: Clonal plasma cell proliferation in bone marrow producing monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein). Causes bone destruction (RANK-L mediated osteoclast activation), renal failure (light chain cast nephropathy), bone marrow failure (anemia), and immunodeficiency (suppression of normal immunoglobulins).
classic_presentation: CRAB criteria: Calcium elevation, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, Bone lesions
key_labs: elevated total protein with low albumin (protein gap)
multiple_myeloma.diagnostic_criteria: ≥10% clonal bone marrow plasma cells OR biopsy-proven plasmacytoma PLUS myeloma-defining event (CRAB criteria or biomarkers: ≥60% plasma cells, free light chain ratio ≥100, >1 focal MRI lesion).
cant_miss_features: spinal cord compression from vertebral fracture/plasmacytoma
multiple_myeloma.typical_course: Median survival improved to 5-7 years with modern therapy (proteasome inhibitors, IMiDs, anti-CD38 antibodies, stem cell transplant). Smoldering myeloma may be observed. Relapse is expected.

polycythemia_vera.epidemiology: Incidence 2 per 100,000/year. Median age 60. Slight male predominance. JAK2 V617F mutation present in >95%.
polycythemia_vera.pathophysiology: Myeloproliferative neoplasm with JAK2 mutation causing erythropoietin-independent erythropoiesis. Increased RBC mass leads to hyperviscosity. Also increased WBC and platelets in many cases. Risk of thrombosis and transformation to myelofibrosis or AML.
key_labs: low MCV (from iron deficiency due to phlebotomy)
polycythemia_vera.diagnostic_criteria: WHO 2016: All 3 major (Hgb >16.5M/16.0F or Hct >49%M/48%F, BM biopsy showing trilineage myeloproliferation, JAK2 mutation) OR first 2 major + minor (low EPO).
mimics: secondary erythrocytosis (COPD, sleep apnea, high altitude, EPO-producing tumors)
polycythemia_vera.typical_course: Median survival >14 years with treatment. Phlebotomy to Hct <45%, low-dose aspirin. Hydroxyurea for high-risk patients. Risk of transformation to post-PV myelofibrosis (10-20% at 20 years) or AML (3-10%).

aplastic_anemia.epidemiology: Rare (2 per million/year in Western countries, higher in Asia). Bimodal age distribution (15-25 and >60). Causes: idiopathic (most), autoimmune, medications (chloramphenicol, NSAIDs, carbamazepine), toxins (benzene), viral (hepatitis, EBV, parvovirus), inherited (Fanconi anemia).
aplastic_anemia.pathophysiology: Immune-mediated (T-cell) destruction of hematopoietic stem cells leading to bone marrow failure and pancytopenia. Bone marrow becomes hypocellular (<25% cellularity) with fat replacement.
aplastic_anemia.diagnostic_criteria: At least 2 of: hemoglobin <10 g/dL, platelets <50,000, neutrophils <1,500 PLUS hypocellular bone marrow (<25% cellularity) with no fibrosis or malignant infiltration. Severe: neutrophils <500, platelets <20,000, reticulocytes <1%.
aplastic_anemia.typical_course: Severe aplastic anemia: fatal within months without treatment. Treatment: allogenic stem cell transplant (young patients with matched donor) or immunosuppressive therapy (ATG + cyclosporine, 70% response rate). Supportive: transfusions, antibiotics, growth factors.

hemochromatosis.epidemiology: Most common genetic disorder in Caucasians. HFE C282Y homozygosity: 1 in 200-300 Northern Europeans. Clinical penetrance much lower (10-25%). Men present earlier (40-60) than women (after menopause).
hemochromatosis.pathophysiology: HFE gene mutations cause decreased hepcidin production leading to unregulated intestinal iron absorption. Iron deposits in hepatocytes, pancreatic islets, cardiac myocytes, joints, and pituitary. Excess iron generates free radicals causing oxidative tissue damage.
hemochromatosis.diagnostic_criteria: Transferrin saturation >45% triggers HFE genotyping. C282Y homozygosity with elevated ferritin confirms. If ferritin >1000 or elevated LFTs, liver biopsy or MRI to assess hepatic iron and fibrosis.
cant_miss_features: cirrhosis with hepatocellular carcinoma risk (200x increased if cirrhotic)
hemochromatosis.typical_course: Phlebotomy treatment highly effective. Target ferritin 50-100 ng/mL. Normal life expectancy if treated before cirrhosis. Arthropathy and hypogonadism may not reverse. HCC screening if cirrhotic.

sickle_cell_disease.epidemiology: ~100,000 Americans affected. Most common in African Americans (1 in 365), also Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, Indian populations. Sickle trait (heterozygous HbAS): 1 in 13 African Americans.
sickle_cell_disease.pathophysiology: Point mutation in beta-globin gene (Glu→Val at position 6) produces hemoglobin S. HbS polymerizes when deoxygenated, deforming RBCs into sickle shape. Sickled cells cause vaso-occlusion (pain crises, organ infarction), chronic hemolytic anemia, and endothelial damage.
sickle_cell_disease.diagnostic_criteria: Hemoglobin electrophoresis: HbSS (most common/severe), HbSC, HbS-beta thalassemia. Newborn screening detects at birth. Sickle solubility test (Sickledex) for rapid screening but does not distinguish HbSS from HbAS.
cant_miss_features: acute chest syndrome (leading cause of death — fever, chest pain, new infiltrate, hypoxia)
splenic sequestration crisis (rapid splenic enlargement, Hgb drop)
sickle_cell_disease.typical_course: Chronic disease with acute exacerbations. Median survival now ~45-55 years. Disease-modifying: hydroxyurea (increases HbF), L-glutamine, crizanlizumab, voxelotor. Curative: allogeneic stem cell transplant, gene therapy emerging.

hepatocellular_injury.epidemiology: Common. Acute causes: viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E), drug-induced (acetaminophen most common), ischemic hepatitis, autoimmune hepatitis. Chronic causes: NAFLD/NASH (most common in Western world), chronic viral hepatitis, alcohol.
hepatocellular_injury.pathophysiology: Hepatocyte damage from direct toxic injury, immune-mediated destruction, or ischemia releases intracellular enzymes (ALT, AST) into blood. ALT is more liver-specific than AST. Pattern of ALT >> AST suggests hepatocellular etiology vs AST >> ALT in alcoholic hepatitis (2:1 ratio).
classic_presentation: AST/ALT markedly elevated (>10x ULN in acute)
hepatocellular_injury.diagnostic_criteria: ALT/AST elevation pattern plus clinical context. Acute liver failure: INR ≥1.5 + encephalopathy. Workup: viral serologies, drug screen, autoimmune markers (ANA, anti-SMA, IgG), ceruloplasmin, iron studies.
cant_miss_features: acute liver failure (encephalopathy + coagulopathy — transfer to transplant center)
Wilson disease (young patient, Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia + liver disease)
hepatocellular_injury.typical_course: Depends on cause. Acetaminophen: NAC highly effective if early. Viral hepatitis A/E: self-limited. Hepatitis B/C: risk of chronicity and cirrhosis. Autoimmune: steroid-responsive. NAFLD/NASH: lifestyle modification, progressive without intervention.

cholestatic_liver_disease.epidemiology: Extrahepatic: gallstones (most common), pancreatic head mass, cholangiocarcinoma. Intrahepatic: primary biliary cholangitis (women 90%, anti-mitochondrial antibodies), primary sclerosing cholangitis (men, associated with IBD), drug-induced (amoxicillin-clavulanate, anabolic steroids).
cholestatic_liver_disease.pathophysiology: Impaired bile flow from intrahepatic or extrahepatic obstruction causes accumulation of bile constituents (bilirubin, bile acids) in liver and blood. ALP and GGT are released from damaged bile duct epithelium. Direct (conjugated) bilirubin elevation predominates.
key_labs: GGT elevated (confirms hepatobiliary origin of ALP)
prolonged PT (vitamin K dependent — corrects with vitamin K unlike hepatocellular disease)
cholestatic_liver_disease.diagnostic_criteria: ALP elevation >1.5x ULN with ALP/ALT ratio suggesting cholestatic pattern. Ultrasound first to assess bile duct dilation (extrahepatic). If dilated: MRCP or ERCP. If non-dilated: AMA (PBC), ANCA (PSC), drug review.
mimics: bone disease (elevated ALP but normal GGT)
cant_miss_features: ascending cholangitis (Charcot triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain — emergency)
cholestatic_liver_disease.typical_course: Depends on cause. Gallstone obstruction: ERCP with stone extraction. PBC: ursodeoxycholic acid slows progression. PSC: no proven medical therapy, liver transplant for end-stage. Drug-induced: remove offending agent.

cirrhosis.epidemiology: End-stage of chronic liver disease. Most common causes: alcohol (30%), NAFLD/NASH (25%), hepatitis C (20%), hepatitis B (15%). Prevalence 600,000-1 million in US. Leading cause of liver transplantation.
cirrhosis.pathophysiology: Chronic hepatic injury causes progressive fibrosis replacing normal parenchyma. Portal hypertension develops from increased intrahepatic resistance. Synthetic failure (albumin, clotting factors) and detoxification failure (ammonia, bilirubin) result. Compensated → decompensated (ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy, jaundice).
key_labs: prolonged PT/INR (does not correct with vitamin K)
cirrhosis.diagnostic_criteria: Clinical features + imaging (nodular liver, ascites, splenomegaly) + labs. FIB-4 and APRI scores for non-invasive fibrosis assessment. Liver biopsy gold standard but often unnecessary. MELD score for severity and transplant listing.
cant_miss_features: hepatocellular carcinoma (screen with ultrasound + AFP every 6 months)
cirrhosis.typical_course: Compensated cirrhosis: median survival 12+ years. Decompensated: median survival 2 years. Each decompensation event worsens prognosis. Liver transplant is curative. MELD score guides transplant prioritization.

wilson_disease.epidemiology: Autosomal recessive. Prevalence 1 in 30,000. ATP7B gene mutation. Typically presents age 5-35. If >40, more likely neuropsychiatric presentation.
wilson_disease.pathophysiology: Impaired biliary copper excretion and impaired incorporation of copper into ceruloplasmin leads to copper accumulation in liver, brain (basal ganglia), cornea (Kayser-Fleischer rings), and kidneys.
classic_presentation: hepatitis or cirrhosis in young patient
key_labs: elevated hepatic copper on biopsy (>250 mcg/g dry weight)
low serum copper (total, but free copper elevated)
wilson_disease.diagnostic_criteria: Leipzig score: Kayser-Fleischer rings, neuropsychiatric symptoms, Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, low ceruloplasmin, elevated urine copper, elevated hepatic copper, ATP7B mutations. Score ≥4 = highly likely.
cant_miss_features: fulminant hepatic failure (often fatal without transplant)
Kayser-Fleischer rings absent in ~50% with hepatic presentation
wilson_disease.typical_course: Without treatment: progressive liver failure and neurologic deterioration, fatal. With treatment (D-penicillamine, trientine, zinc): hepatic disease stabilizes/improves. Neurologic symptoms may worsen initially before improving. Liver transplant curative for fulminant or end-stage.

autoimmune_hepatitis.epidemiology: Prevalence ~17 per 100,000. Female predominance (4:1). Type 1 (ANA/anti-SMA positive): any age. Type 2 (anti-LKM1 positive): mainly children/young adults. Often associated with other autoimmune diseases.
autoimmune_hepatitis.pathophysiology: T-cell mediated destruction of hepatocytes with interface hepatitis on biopsy. Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3, DR4) plus environmental triggers. Characterized by elevated IgG and autoantibodies.
classic_presentation: may present as acute hepatitis or insidious chronic disease
autoimmune_hepatitis.diagnostic_criteria: Simplified IAIHG criteria: autoantibodies (ANA, SMA ≥1:40; LKM1 ≥1:40), IgG (>ULN), liver histology (compatible/typical), absence of viral hepatitis. Score ≥7 = definite, 6 = probable.
cant_miss_features: steroid-responsive — delayed treatment leads to cirrhosis
autoimmune_hepatitis.typical_course: Without treatment: 40% mortality at 6 months in severe cases, 10-year survival 27%. With immunosuppression (prednisone + azathioprine): 80% achieve remission, 10-year survival >90%. Relapse common when immunosuppression withdrawn.

chronic_kidney_disease.epidemiology: Prevalence ~15% of US adults (37 million). Leading causes: diabetes (44%), hypertension (28%), glomerulonephritis (7%). More common in elderly, African Americans, and those with family history.
chronic_kidney_disease.pathophysiology: Progressive loss of nephrons from any cause leads to reduced GFR, retention of uremic toxins, impaired acid-base/electrolyte regulation, and loss of endocrine functions (EPO, active vitamin D). Remaining nephrons hyperfiltrate, accelerating injury. CKD stages by GFR: G1 ≥90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15.
chronic_kidney_disease.diagnostic_criteria: GFR <60 mL/min/1.73m2 OR markers of kidney damage (albuminuria ACR ≥30 mg/g, urine sediment abnormalities, electrolyte abnormalities, structural abnormalities, history of transplant) for >3 months.
cant_miss_features: accelerated cardiovascular disease (leading cause of death in CKD)
chronic_kidney_disease.typical_course: Progressive, rate depends on underlying cause and control of risk factors. Goal: slow progression with ACE inhibitor/ARB, BP control, glycemic control, SGLT2 inhibitors. Stage 5 (ESRD): dialysis or transplant. Transplant offers best survival.

nephrotic_syndrome.epidemiology: Children: minimal change disease most common (77%). Adults: membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), diabetic nephropathy. Secondary causes: diabetes, SLE, amyloidosis, medications (NSAIDs).
nephrotic_syndrome.pathophysiology: Damaged glomerular filtration barrier (podocyte injury) allows massive protein loss in urine (>3.5 g/day). Hypoalbuminemia reduces oncotic pressure causing edema. Liver compensatory protein synthesis includes lipoproteins (hyperlipidemia) and clotting factors (hypercoagulability). Loss of antithrombin III and immunoglobulins increases thrombosis and infection risk.
nephrotic_syndrome.diagnostic_criteria: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/day or urine protein:creatinine ratio >3.5) + hypoalbuminemia + edema. Kidney biopsy for diagnosis in adults (children: empiric steroids for presumed minimal change disease first).
nephrotic_syndrome.typical_course: Minimal change disease: excellent prognosis, 90% remission with steroids, frequent relapses. Membranous: 30% spontaneous remission, risk of progression. FSGS: often steroid-resistant, progressive. Diabetic nephropathy: progressive, ACEi/ARB and SGLT2i slow progression.

nephritic_syndrome.epidemiology: Post-streptococcal GN: children 5-12 years (most common in developing countries). IgA nephropathy: most common GN worldwide, young males. Lupus nephritis: follows SLE demographics. ANCA vasculitis: older adults.
nephritic_syndrome.pathophysiology: Inflammatory damage to glomeruli causing disruption of glomerular basement membrane. Red blood cells, white blood cells, and protein leak into urine. Decreased GFR causes fluid retention (edema, hypertension) and azotemia. Complement activation and immune complex deposition are common mechanisms.
nephritic_syndrome.diagnostic_criteria: Hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs/RBC casts) + proteinuria + hypertension + edema + azotemia. Kidney biopsy essential for diagnosis and classification (except post-streptococcal GN in children with classic presentation).
mimics: IgA nephropathy (may present as isolated hematuria)
cant_miss_features: rapidly progressive GN (RPGN — crescentic GN requiring urgent immunosuppression)
nephritic_syndrome.typical_course: Post-streptococcal: self-limited in children (95% recover). IgA nephropathy: variable, 20-40% progress to ESRD over 20 years. Lupus nephritis: depends on class (III/IV require aggressive immunosuppression). ANCA vasculitis: cyclophosphamide/rituximab induction.

renal_tubular_acidosis.epidemiology: Type 1 (distal): autoimmune (Sjogren's), medications (amphotericin B, lithium), hereditary. Type 2 (proximal): Fanconi syndrome, multiple myeloma, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors. Type 4 (hyperkalemic): diabetes, aldosterone deficiency/resistance, ACEi/ARB/K-sparing diuretics.
renal_tubular_acidosis.pathophysiology: Type 1: impaired distal H+ secretion → inability to acidify urine (pH >5.5), hypokalemia, nephrocalcinosis. Type 2: impaired proximal bicarbonate reabsorption → bicarbonate wasting, hypokalemia. Type 4: aldosterone deficiency/resistance → hyperkalemia, mild acidosis.
classic_presentation: non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (normal anion gap)
Type 1: kidney stones, nephrocalcinosis, growth failure in children
Type 2: osteomalacia, rickets in children, Fanconi syndrome features
Type 4: often asymptomatic, mild acidosis with hyperkalemia
key_labs: Type 1: urine pH >5.5, hypokalemia, positive urine anion gap
Type 2: urine pH <5.5 (can acidify when bicarb low), hypokalemia, fractional excretion of bicarbonate >15%
Type 4: hyperkalemia, urine pH <5.5, low transtubular potassium gradient, low aldosterone/renin
renal_tubular_acidosis.diagnostic_criteria: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis + urine anion gap (positive = renal cause) + urine pH + serum potassium + clinical context. Urine pH >5.5 with metabolic acidosis suggests Type 1. Hyperkalemia suggests Type 4.
mimics: diarrhea (non-anion gap acidosis but urine anion gap negative)
cant_miss_features: Type 1: nephrocalcinosis and progressive renal failure if untreated
renal_tubular_acidosis.typical_course: Type 1: oral bicarbonate/citrate supplementation, monitor for nephrocalcinosis. Type 2: high-dose bicarbonate (often wasted in urine), treat underlying cause. Type 4: treat hyperkalemia, fludrocortisone if aldosterone-deficient, dietary potassium restriction.

systemic_lupus_erythematosus.epidemiology: Prevalence ~20-150 per 100,000. Women:men 9:1. Peak onset 15-45 years. More common and severe in African Americans, Hispanics, Asians. Genetic (HLA-DR2, DR3) + environmental (UV light, infections, estrogen) triggers.
systemic_lupus_erythematosus.pathophysiology: Loss of immune tolerance to self-antigens with production of pathogenic autoantibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith). Immune complex deposition in tissues causes complement activation and inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, serosal surfaces, blood cells, and CNS.
systemic_lupus_erythematosus.diagnostic_criteria: 2019 EULAR/ACR criteria: ANA ≥1:80 as entry criterion, then score clinical domains (constitutional, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal) and immunologic domains (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, antiphospholipid, complement, direct Coombs). Score ≥10 = SLE.
cant_miss_features: lupus nephritis (class III/IV — requires renal biopsy and aggressive immunosuppression)
systemic_lupus_erythematosus.typical_course: Relapsing-remitting. 10-year survival >90% with modern treatment. Treatment: hydroxychloroquine (all patients), NSAIDs for mild disease, steroids + immunosuppressants (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, belimumab) for organ-threatening. Leading causes of death: infection, cardiovascular disease, renal failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.epidemiology: Prevalence 0.5-1%. Women:men 3:1. Peak onset 40-60 years. Risk factors: smoking, family history (HLA-DR4), female sex.
rheumatoid_arthritis.pathophysiology: Autoimmune synovitis with pannus formation causing cartilage and bone erosion. CD4+ T cells, macrophages, and fibroblast-like synoviocytes produce TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6 driving chronic inflammation. Citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA/anti-CCP) are pathogenic.
classic_presentation: symmetric polyarthritis (MCP, PIP, wrist, MTP joints)
extra-articular: interstitial lung disease, pericarditis, scleritis, vasculitis
key_labs: joint X-rays: periarticular osteopenia, marginal erosions, joint space narrowing
rheumatoid_arthritis.diagnostic_criteria: 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria: score based on joint involvement (≥1 large joint to >10 small joints), serology (RF, anti-CCP), acute-phase reactants (ESR, CRP), duration (≥6 weeks). Score ≥6/10 = definite RA.
cant_miss_features: cervical spine instability (atlantoaxial subluxation — flexion/extension views before intubation)
rheumatoid_arthritis.typical_course: Progressive joint destruction without treatment. Treat-to-target strategy: methotrexate first-line DMARD, escalate to biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, JAK inhibitors) if inadequate response. Early aggressive treatment prevents erosions. Disease remission achievable in 30-50%.

tumor_lysis_syndrome.epidemiology: Risk factors: high tumor burden, rapidly proliferating tumors (Burkitt lymphoma highest risk, ALL, AML), high WBC count, pre-existing renal insufficiency, elevated baseline LDH/uric acid. Can be spontaneous or treatment-induced.
tumor_lysis_syndrome.pathophysiology: Rapid lysis of tumor cells releases intracellular contents: potassium (hyperkalemia), phosphorus (hyperphosphatemia → calcium phosphate precipitation → hypocalcemia), nucleic acids (metabolized to uric acid → hyperuricemia → uric acid nephropathy). Acute kidney injury from uric acid and calcium phosphate crystal deposition in tubules.
tumor_lysis_syndrome.diagnostic_criteria: Cairo-Bishop: Laboratory TLS: ≥2 lab abnormalities within 3 days before to 7 days after chemo. Clinical TLS: lab TLS + renal insufficiency, cardiac arrhythmia, seizure, or death.
mimics: acute kidney injury from other causes
tumor_lysis_syndrome.typical_course: Prevention: aggressive IV hydration, allopurinol (moderate risk) or rasburicase (high risk), avoid potassium and phosphorus in fluids. Treatment: rasburicase for uric acid, kayexalate/insulin-glucose for potassium, phosphate binders, dialysis if refractory. Mortality 10-20% once established.

hypercalcemia_of_malignancy.epidemiology: Occurs in 10-30% of cancer patients. Most common: squamous cell cancers (lung, head/neck), breast, renal, multiple myeloma. PTHrP-mediated (80%), osteolytic metastases (20%), 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production (lymphomas, <1%).
hypercalcemia_of_malignancy.pathophysiology: PTHrP mimics PTH action: increases bone resorption and renal calcium reabsorption. Unlike primary hyperparathyroidism, PTH itself is suppressed. In myeloma/breast: direct bone destruction by metastases with local cytokine release. In lymphomas: tumor 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production.
key_labs: suppressed PTH (key distinction from primary hyperparathyroidism)
hypercalcemia_of_malignancy.diagnostic_criteria: Hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH in setting of known or suspected malignancy. Elevated PTHrP confirms humoral mechanism. If PTH elevated, consider concurrent primary hyperparathyroidism.
hypercalcemia_of_malignancy.typical_course: Treatment: aggressive IV normal saline, calcitonin (rapid onset, tachyphylaxis), zoledronic acid or denosumab (onset 2-4 days), treat underlying malignancy. Prognosis poor — 30-day mortality ~50%. Recurrence unless underlying cancer controlled.

acute_myocardial_infarction.epidemiology: Leading cause of death worldwide. Risk factors: age (men >45, women >55), hypertension, diabetes, smoking, dyslipidemia, family history, obesity. Annual incidence ~550,000 new + ~200,000 recurrent in US.
acute_myocardial_infarction.pathophysiology: STEMI: complete thrombotic occlusion of coronary artery, usually from ruptured atherosclerotic plaque. NSTEMI: subtotal occlusion or microembolization. Myocardial necrosis from prolonged ischemia. Type 1 MI (plaque rupture) vs Type 2 MI (supply-demand mismatch).
classic_presentation: radiation to left arm, jaw, back
atypical: women/diabetics/elderly may present with fatigue, dyspnea, or epigastric discomfort without chest pain
key_labs: elevated troponin I or T (rise and fall pattern)
ECG: ST elevation (STEMI), ST depression/T-wave inversion (NSTEMI)
acute_myocardial_infarction.diagnostic_criteria: Rise and/or fall of cardiac troponin (at least one value above 99th percentile URL) PLUS at least one of: ischemic symptoms, new ischemic ECG changes, new pathological Q waves, imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium, intracoronary thrombus on angiography.
cant_miss_features: STEMI requires emergent PCI within 90 minutes (door-to-balloon)
mechanical complications (free wall rupture, VSD, papillary muscle rupture)
acute_myocardial_infarction.typical_course: STEMI: emergent PCI or fibrinolytics. NSTEMI: risk-stratified approach to invasive strategy. Medical therapy: dual antiplatelet therapy, statin, beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB. Cardiac rehab. In-hospital mortality: STEMI 5-7%, NSTEMI 3-5%. Long-term: secondary prevention reduces recurrent events.

heart_failure.epidemiology: Prevalence 6.2 million US adults. Incidence increases with age. HFrEF (EF ≤40%): CAD most common cause. HFpEF (EF ≥50%): hypertension, obesity, aging. Risk factors: hypertension, CAD, diabetes, obesity, valvular disease, cardiotoxic drugs.
heart_failure.pathophysiology: HFrEF: reduced contractility (systolic failure) from myocardial damage. Neurohormonal activation (RAAS, sympathetic) initially compensatory but ultimately maladaptive (remodeling, fibrosis). HFpEF: impaired relaxation/filling (diastolic failure) from myocardial stiffness. Both lead to congestion and inadequate cardiac output.
key_labs: elevated BNP (>100 pg/mL) or NT-proBNP (>300 pg/mL)
chest X-ray: cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema, pleural effusions
echocardiography: reduced or preserved EF, diastolic dysfunction, wall motion abnormalities
heart_failure.diagnostic_criteria: Framingham criteria: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor criteria. Major: PND, JVD, rales, cardiomegaly, acute pulmonary edema, S3, hepatojugular reflux. Minor: ankle edema, night cough, DOE, hepatomegaly, pleural effusion, tachycardia. BNP/NT-proBNP to confirm.
cant_miss_features: acute decompensation with pulmonary edema (emergent treatment)
flash pulmonary edema (consider renal artery stenosis)
cardiac amyloidosis (HFpEF with thickened walls, low voltage ECG)
heart_failure.typical_course: Chronic, progressive. HFrEF GDMT: ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor — all improve mortality. Device therapy: ICD if EF ≤35%, CRT if LBBB + wide QRS. Stage D: mechanical support (LVAD) or transplant. 5-year mortality ~50%. HFpEF: diuretics for congestion, SGLT2 inhibitors, manage comorbidities.

myocarditis.epidemiology: Often underdiagnosed. Most common cause: viral (coxsackievirus B, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, SARS-CoV-2). Also autoimmune, drug-induced (immune checkpoint inhibitors), giant cell myocarditis (fulminant). Young adults, male predominance.
myocarditis.pathophysiology: Viral myocardial invasion with direct cytotoxicity and/or immune-mediated myocyte damage. Inflammation causes myocyte necrosis, edema, and potential electrical instability. May progress to dilated cardiomyopathy. Giant cell myocarditis: aggressive lymphocytic infiltration with multinucleated giant cells.
key_labs: ECG: diffuse ST changes, arrhythmias, new conduction abnormalities
cardiac MRI: late gadolinium enhancement (non-ischemic pattern), myocardial edema (T2-weighted)
echocardiography: reduced EF, regional wall motion abnormalities
viral PCR if endomyocardial biopsy performed
myocarditis.diagnostic_criteria: Clinical presentation (symptoms + troponin elevation + ECG/echo abnormalities) + cardiac MRI (Lake Louise criteria: T2 edema + T1 late gadolinium enhancement). Endomyocardial biopsy: gold standard but rarely performed except in fulminant/refractory cases.
cant_miss_features: fulminant myocarditis (rapid cardiogenic shock — may need mechanical circulatory support)
giant cell myocarditis (requires immunosuppression, often transplant)
avoid NSAIDs in acute phase (animal data suggest worsened outcomes)
myocarditis.typical_course: Most viral myocarditis self-limited with recovery of LV function over weeks-months. ~15-20% progress to dilated cardiomyopathy. Exercise restriction for 3-6 months. Fulminant: paradoxically better long-term prognosis if survived (bridge to recovery). Giant cell: aggressive, high mortality without transplant.

rhabdomyolysis.epidemiology: Causes: trauma/crush injury, extreme exertion, statins + fibrates, seizures, ischemia (compartment syndrome), drugs/toxins (cocaine, alcohol), hyperthermia (NMS, malignant hyperthermia), infections, metabolic myopathies. ~26,000 cases/year in US.
rhabdomyolysis.pathophysiology: Skeletal muscle necrosis releases intracellular contents: CK (diagnostic marker), myoglobin (nephrotoxic), potassium, phosphorus, uric acid, LDH. Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules causing pigment nephropathy. Acute kidney injury from tubular obstruction, direct tubular toxicity, and renal vasoconstriction.
classic_presentation: dark brown urine (myoglobinuria — dipstick positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy)
key_labs: CK markedly elevated (>5x ULN, often >10,000 U/L)
hypocalcemia (early — calcium deposits in damaged muscle)
urine dipstick positive for blood, negative microscopy for RBCs
rhabdomyolysis.diagnostic_criteria: CK >5x ULN (typically >5,000 U/L) in appropriate clinical context. CK >15,000-20,000 U/L associated with high AKI risk. Myoglobin less useful (short half-life, may have cleared).
rhabdomyolysis.typical_course: Treatment: aggressive IV normal saline (target UOP 200-300 mL/hr), treat hyperkalemia, avoid nephrotoxins, consider bicarbonate for urine alkalinization (controversial). CK peaks at 24-72 hours, declines 40%/day. AKI usually recoverable. Mortality ~5% overall, higher with AKI and comorbidities.

methanol_ethylene_glycol_poisoning.epidemiology: Methanol: found in windshield washer fluid, fuel additives, moonshine. Ethylene glycol: found in antifreeze, de-icing solutions. Both can be ingested accidentally (children) or intentionally (suicide, alcohol substitution). Lethal dose: methanol ~1 mL/kg, ethylene glycol ~1.5 mL/kg.
methanol_ethylene_glycol_poisoning.pathophysiology: Parent compounds have low toxicity. Alcohol dehydrogenase converts them to toxic metabolites. Methanol → formaldehyde → formic acid (retinal toxicity, optic nerve damage). Ethylene glycol → glycolaldehyde → glycolic acid → oxalic acid (renal tubular damage, calcium oxalate crystal deposition). Both cause severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis.
classic_presentation: Methanol: visual disturbances (blurred vision, blindness), headache, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, CNS depression
Ethylene glycol: stages — CNS (inebriation, 0-12h) → cardiopulmonary (tachycardia, pulmonary edema, 12-24h) → renal (flank pain, oliguria, AKI, 24-72h)
Both: inebriation early, then severe metabolic acidosis
key_labs: severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis
elevated methanol or ethylene glycol level
calcium oxalate crystals in urine (ethylene glycol)
methanol_ethylene_glycol_poisoning.diagnostic_criteria: High anion gap metabolic acidosis + elevated osmolal gap + appropriate exposure history. Specific serum levels confirm. Treatment should not be delayed for levels if clinical suspicion high.
mimics: isopropanol ingestion (elevated osmolal gap but NO acidosis)
cant_miss_features: permanent blindness (methanol — damage irreversible if treatment delayed)
must treat empirically if suspected (do not wait for levels)
methanol_ethylene_glycol_poisoning.typical_course: Treatment: fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) or ethanol if fomepizole unavailable. Hemodialysis for severe acidosis (pH <7.15), renal failure, visual symptoms, serum level >50 mg/dL. Folate for methanol (enhances formate metabolism). Pyridoxine/thiamine for ethylene glycol. Prognosis good with early treatment.

lactic_acidosis.epidemiology: Type A (hypoxic, most common): shock, sepsis, cardiac arrest, severe anemia, CO poisoning, mesenteric ischemia. Type B (non-hypoxic): liver failure, malignancy (Warburg effect), medications (metformin, NRTIs, linezolid), thiamine deficiency, mitochondrial myopathies. D-lactic acidosis: short bowel syndrome.
lactic_acidosis.pathophysiology: Type A: tissue hypoperfusion or hypoxemia causes anaerobic glycolysis with pyruvate reduction to lactate. Normal lactate production ~1500 mmol/day, cleared by liver/kidneys. In shock, production exceeds clearance. Type B: impaired mitochondrial function or hepatic clearance without tissue hypoxia.
key_labs: elevated lactate (>2 mmol/L abnormal, >4 mmol/L severe)
specific to cause: elevated creatinine, liver enzymes, etc.
lactic_acidosis.diagnostic_criteria: Lactate >2 mmol/L with metabolic acidosis. Type A vs B distinguished by clinical context (presence or absence of tissue hypoperfusion).
mimics: other causes of high anion gap acidosis (ketoacidosis, uremia, toxic ingestion)
D-lactic acidosis (not detected by standard L-lactate assay)
cant_miss_features: lactate >4 mmol/L in sepsis associated with high mortality
mesenteric ischemia (severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam)
lactic_acidosis.typical_course: Treat underlying cause. Type A: restore perfusion (fluids, vasopressors, transfusion). Metformin-associated: hold metformin, hemodialysis. Lactate clearance is prognostic — failure to clear by 50% in 6 hours associated with poor outcome. Bicarbonate therapy controversial (may worsen intracellular acidosis).

sepsis.epidemiology: 1.7 million cases/year in US. Mortality 15-30% (sepsis), 40-50% (septic shock). Risk factors: extremes of age, immunosuppression, diabetes, chronic organ disease, invasive devices, recent surgery. Most common sources: pneumonia (35%), intra-abdominal (25%), UTI (15%).
sepsis.pathophysiology: Dysregulated host immune response to infection causing organ dysfunction. Pathogen-associated molecular patterns activate innate immunity → cytokine storm (TNF, IL-1, IL-6) → endothelial dysfunction → vasodilation (distributive shock) → microvascular thrombosis → organ ischemia. Coagulopathy (DIC) may develop.
sepsis.diagnostic_criteria: Sepsis-3 (2016): suspected infection + acute increase in SOFA score ≥2. qSOFA (screening): altered mental status, systolic BP ≤100, respiratory rate ≥22 (≥2 = further assessment). Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressors needed for MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 despite adequate fluids.
mimics: SIRS from non-infectious cause (pancreatitis, burns, trauma)
cant_miss_features: 1-hour bundle: blood cultures, lactate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV fluids (30 mL/kg crystalloid), vasopressors if hypotensive after fluids
source control (drain abscess, remove infected device)
sepsis.typical_course: Early goal-directed therapy improved outcomes. Mortality increases 4-8% per hour of delayed antibiotics. Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines: cultures before antibiotics, empiric broad-spectrum therapy, de-escalate based on cultures. Monitor lactate clearance. ICU care for organ support. Survivors may have long-term cognitive impairment and functional decline.

infective_endocarditis.epidemiology: Incidence 3-10 per 100,000/year. Risk factors: prosthetic valves, previous endocarditis, congenital heart disease, IV drug use (right-sided), poor dentition, hemodialysis. Causative organisms: Staphylococcus aureus (most common overall and in IVDU), viridans streptococci (native valve), enterococci, HACEK organisms.
infective_endocarditis.pathophysiology: Bacteria adhere to damaged or prosthetic endocardium forming infected vegetations (fibrin-platelet-bacterial mass). Continuous bacteremia. Complications from: local tissue destruction (valve destruction, abscess), embolic events (stroke, septic pulmonary emboli, mycotic aneurysms), immune complex deposition (glomerulonephritis, vasculitis).
classic_presentation: constitutional symptoms (fatigue, malaise, weight loss, night sweats)
embolic phenomena: stroke, Janeway lesions (painless palmar/plantar macules), splinter hemorrhages
immunologic phenomena: Osler nodes (painful fingertip nodules), Roth spots (retinal hemorrhages), glomerulonephritis
IV drug use: right-sided (tricuspid) with septic pulmonary emboli
key_labs: positive blood cultures (sustained bacteremia — at least 2 sets positive)
echocardiography: vegetations, abscess, new valvular regurgitation (TEE superior to TTE, sensitivity 90-95% vs 50-60%)
infective_endocarditis.diagnostic_criteria: Modified Duke criteria: DEFINITE = 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor. Major: (1) positive blood cultures (typical organism in ≥2 cultures, or persistently positive), (2) endocardial involvement on echo (vegetation, abscess, new dehiscence, new regurgitation). Minor: predisposition, fever, vascular phenomena, immunologic phenomena, supportive micro/echo.
mimics: non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (Libman-Sacks in SLE, marantic in malignancy)
cant_miss_features: embolic stroke (common presentation of S. aureus endocarditis)
heart failure from acute valve destruction (surgical emergency)
prosthetic valve endocarditis (higher mortality, often surgical)
infective_endocarditis.typical_course: IV antibiotics 4-6 weeks. Native valve streptococcal: excellent prognosis with antibiotics. S. aureus: higher mortality (25-40%), more embolic events, more likely to need surgery. Indications for surgery: heart failure, uncontrolled infection, large vegetations (>10mm) with embolic events, abscess. Prosthetic valve: lower threshold for surgery. Overall mortality 15-30%.

acute_pancreatitis.epidemiology: Incidence ~35 per 100,000/year (increasing). Causes: gallstones (40%), alcohol (30%), hypertriglyceridemia (>1000 mg/dL), ERCP, medications (azathioprine, valproic acid), idiopathic (15-25%). Peak age 30-70.
acute_pancreatitis.pathophysiology: Premature activation of pancreatic enzymes (trypsinogen → trypsin) within acinar cells causing autodigestion. Inflammatory cascade leads to local (peripancreatic necrosis, fluid collections) and systemic (SIRS, organ failure) complications. Severe pancreatitis: necrotizing with multi-organ failure.
classic_presentation: severe epigastric pain radiating to back
better sitting up and leaning forward
Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) and Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis) in severe hemorrhagic pancreatitis
key_labs: lipase elevated >3x ULN (more sensitive and specific than amylase)
elevated CRP (>150 mg/L at 48h predicts severe)
acute_pancreatitis.diagnostic_criteria: Requires ≥2 of 3: (1) characteristic abdominal pain, (2) lipase/amylase >3x ULN, (3) characteristic imaging findings (CT, MRI, or ultrasound). Severity: Revised Atlanta classification — mild (no organ failure, no local complications), moderate (transient organ failure <48h), severe (persistent organ failure >48h).
cant_miss_features: infected pancreatic necrosis (usually after week 2, fever + worsening despite supportive care — FNA for Gram stain)
acute_pancreatitis.typical_course: Mild (80%): self-limited, resolves in 3-5 days with supportive care (IV fluids, pain control, early oral feeding). Severe (20%): ICU admission, early organ failure (first 2 weeks), late infected necrosis (weeks 2-6). Mortality: mild <1%, severe 15-30%. Biliary: cholecystectomy during same admission. Alcohol: abstinence counseling.

celiac_disease.epidemiology: Prevalence ~1% worldwide. Peak diagnosis: childhood and 30-50 years. Strongly associated with HLA-DQ2 (95%) and HLA-DQ8. Risk factors: family history (10% first-degree relatives), other autoimmune diseases (type 1 diabetes, thyroiditis, IgA deficiency).
celiac_disease.pathophysiology: Immune-mediated enteropathy triggered by gluten (gliadin) in genetically susceptible individuals. Tissue transglutaminase deamidates gliadin, creating neoepitopes presented by HLA-DQ2/DQ8 to T cells. Resulting immune response damages small bowel villous architecture (villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia), causing malabsorption.
classic_presentation: iron deficiency anemia (refractory to oral iron)
many patients have subtle or extra-intestinal symptoms only
key_labs: deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) IgG (use if IgA deficient)
total IgA (exclude IgA deficiency, 2-3% of celiac patients)
celiac_disease.diagnostic_criteria: Positive serology (tTG-IgA) while on gluten-containing diet + confirmatory duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy (Marsh classification). In children with tTG-IgA >10x ULN + positive EMA + symptoms, biopsy may be deferred (ESPGHAN 2020). Clinical and serologic response to gluten-free diet supports diagnosis.
cant_miss_features: refractory celiac disease (may harbor enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma)
must check for IgA deficiency (false negative serology)
celiac_disease.typical_course: Strict lifelong gluten-free diet leads to symptomatic improvement in weeks, mucosal healing in months-years. Serologies normalize. Non-responsive: assess adherence (most common cause), consider refractory celiac disease. Associated conditions: type 1 diabetes, thyroid disease, IgA deficiency. Small increased risk of T-cell lymphoma and adenocarcinoma of small bowel.

preclinical_sle.epidemiology: Preclinical phase may precede full SLE by years. Same demographics as SLE: women 9:1, peak 15-45, more common in African Americans, Hispanics, Asians. ANA positivity may precede clinical disease by >5 years.
preclinical_sle.pathophysiology: Early autoimmune dysregulation with autoantibody production and subtle complement consumption before sufficient immune complex deposition to cause overt organ damage. Characterized by individually borderline lab values that collectively suggest evolving autoimmunity.
classic_presentation: may be entirely asymptomatic with incidental lab findings
key_labs: complement C3/C4 at low-normal or mildly decreased
preclinical_sle.diagnostic_criteria: Does not yet meet full EULAR/ACR 2019 SLE criteria (score <10). Requires longitudinal monitoring. Accumulation of autoantibodies and subtle lab trends over serial measurements. Transition to classifiable SLE occurs in 10-50% depending on baseline autoantibody profile.
preclinical_sle.typical_course: Variable. Some remain in preclinical phase indefinitely (incomplete lupus). Others accumulate criteria over months-years and develop classifiable SLE. Hydroxychloroquine may delay or prevent progression. Regular monitoring every 3-6 months recommended.

nephrotic_syndrome_minimal_change.epidemiology: Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (77%). Less common in adults (10-15%). Associated with atopy, NSAIDs, lithium, Hodgkin lymphoma. Peak age in children: 2-6 years.
nephrotic_syndrome_minimal_change.pathophysiology: Podocyte foot process effacement (seen on electron microscopy) without inflammatory changes on light microscopy. Likely T-cell mediated circulating permeability factor damages podocyte slit diaphragms. Selective proteinuria (primarily albumin).
classic_presentation: often preceded by URI or allergic reaction
nephrotic_syndrome_minimal_change.diagnostic_criteria: Children: empiric steroid trial without biopsy (steroid response confirms diagnosis). Adults: kidney biopsy showing normal light microscopy, negative immunofluorescence, foot process effacement on EM.
cant_miss_features: steroid-dependent or frequently relapsing course (consider FSGS on biopsy)
spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in children with ascites
nephrotic_syndrome_minimal_change.typical_course: Children: >90% respond to steroids within 4-8 weeks. 60% relapse. Most children outgrow by adolescence. Adults: 80% respond but more slowly (8-16 weeks). Steroid-sparing agents (cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors, rituximab) for frequent relapsers.

gout.epidemiology: Prevalence ~4% of US adults. Men >> women (until menopause). Peak onset 40-60 in men, post-menopause in women. Risk factors: hyperuricemia, obesity, alcohol (beer), purine-rich diet, CKD, diuretics, organ transplant (cyclosporine).
gout.pathophysiology: Monosodium urate (MSU) crystal deposition in joints from chronic hyperuricemia (serum urate >6.8 mg/dL). Crystal phagocytosis by neutrophils triggers NLRP3 inflammasome activation and IL-1beta release causing intense acute inflammation. Chronic tophaceous gout: granulomatous inflammation around crystal deposits.
classic_presentation: acute onset monoarthritis (first MTP most classic = podagra)
tophi in chronic disease (ear helix, olecranon bursa, Achilles tendon)
key_labs: serum uric acid often >7 mg/dL (may be normal during acute flare)
synovial fluid: needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals (definitive)
gout.diagnostic_criteria: Gold standard: MSU crystals identified in synovial fluid or tophus aspirate by polarized microscopy. 2015 ACR/EULAR classification criteria (clinical scoring) if aspiration not feasible. Dual-energy CT can identify urate deposits.
mimics: septic arthritis (MUST exclude by joint aspiration — can coexist with gout)
pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate — positively birefringent rhomboid crystals)
cant_miss_features: septic arthritis must be excluded (can coexist with gout)
do NOT start allopurinol during acute flare (worsens attack)
chronic kidney disease from urate nephropathy
gout.typical_course: Acute flares: colchicine (within 36h of onset), NSAIDs, or steroids. Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat) for recurrent flares (≥2/year), tophi, CKD, or urolithiasis. Target serum urate <6 mg/dL. Must provide anti-inflammatory prophylaxis when starting ULT (colchicine for 3-6 months). With adherent ULT, tophi resolve and flares cease.

pulmonary_embolism.epidemiology: Third most common cardiovascular cause of death. Incidence ~60-70 per 100,000/year. Risk factors (Virchow's triad): stasis (immobilization, long travel), endothelial injury (surgery, trauma), hypercoagulability (cancer, OCP, pregnancy, factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid syndrome).
pulmonary_embolism.pathophysiology: Thrombus (usually from deep leg veins) embolizes to pulmonary vasculature. Obstruction increases pulmonary vascular resistance and right ventricular afterload. Large PE: acute right heart failure and hemodynamic collapse. V/Q mismatch causes hypoxemia. In situ thrombosis can also occur.
key_labs: ECG: sinus tachycardia (most common), S1Q3T3 (classic but uncommon), right heart strain
CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) — gold standard imaging
pulmonary_embolism.diagnostic_criteria: Wells criteria or Geneva score for pretest probability. Low probability + negative D-dimer = PE excluded. Moderate/high probability: CTPA. Massive PE: consider bedside echocardiography showing RV dilation/dysfunction.
cant_miss_features: massive PE with hemodynamic instability (systemic thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy)
pulmonary_embolism.typical_course: Anticoagulation: heparin bridge to warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs — rivaroxaban, apixaban preferred). Duration: provoked 3 months, unprovoked 3-6 months then reassess indefinite. Massive PE: systemic thrombolysis (alteplase), surgical embolectomy, or catheter-directed therapy. Mortality: overall 2-8%, massive PE 25-50%.

deep_vein_thrombosis.epidemiology: Incidence ~100 per 100,000/year. Same risk factors as PE (Virchow's triad). ~50% of proximal DVTs will embolize to lungs if untreated. Recurrence rate 30% at 10 years.
deep_vein_thrombosis.pathophysiology: Thrombus formation in deep veins, usually lower extremities (iliac, femoral, popliteal). Initiated by venous stasis, endothelial injury, or hypercoagulable state. Proximal DVT (above knee) carries higher PE risk than distal (calf) DVT.
classic_presentation: phlegmasia cerulea dolens (massive — blue, painful, swollen leg with arterial compromise)
key_labs: CT venography or MR venography if ultrasound equivocal
deep_vein_thrombosis.diagnostic_criteria: Wells score for DVT: pretest probability. Low probability + negative D-dimer = DVT excluded. Moderate/high probability: compression ultrasound. If negative but high clinical suspicion: repeat in 5-7 days or CT/MR venography.
cant_miss_features: concurrent PE (present in ~50% of proximal DVT)
upper extremity DVT (Paget-Schroetter syndrome, central lines)
deep_vein_thrombosis.typical_course: Anticoagulation same as PE. Proximal DVT: anticoagulate immediately. Isolated distal DVT: may observe with serial ultrasound or anticoagulate. Compression stockings no longer routinely recommended. Post-thrombotic syndrome (chronic pain, swelling, skin changes) develops in 20-50%. IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated.
